title: Reading Practice (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading comprehension drills, one lesson per VOA Learning English passage.

content.introduction: Read "Be Careful What You 'Bank On'" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Banks help us manage our money. When we make deposits, we put money in; when we make withdrawals, we take money out.
prompt.question: What do banks help us do?

prompt.passage: If you bank on something, you trust it will happen. For example, you can bank on the fact that a famous person caught doing something wrong will soon give a public apology.
prompt.question: What does "bank on" mean?

prompt.passage: When we have money in the bank, we count on it to be there when we need it. Banks offer guarantees that they will protect our money.
prompt.question: What do banks guarantee?

prompt.passage: If you laugh all the way to the bank, you make a lot of money with very little effort.
prompt.question: What does "laugh all the way to the bank" mean?
options: make a lot of money easily

answer_key.answer: make a lot of money easily

prompt.passage: When a woman started an organic homemade dog food business, her friends thought it was a silly idea. But it ended up being very successful and made her very rich.
prompt.question: What happened to the woman's business?

content.introduction: Read "The Story of Jack Frost" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Jack Frost represents the coming of dark, cold days. He is the personification of winter, a human form of winter weather. So, when we say, “Jack Frost is nipping at your nose,” we mean that you feel the cold, icy effects of winter on your face.
prompt.question: What does the phrase "Jack Frost is nipping at your nose" mean?
options: Jack Frost is playing with your nose
You feel the cold of winter on your face
Jack Frost is tickling your nose
It is hot around your nose

answer_key.answer: You feel the cold of winter on your face

prompt.passage: When a frost or deep freeze comes, you had better bundle up! You should wear enough clothing to stay warm.
But he can also create beauty in nature. Some traditions claim that Jack Frost is responsible for leaving ice and snow on windows, trees, the ground…anywhere it will stick.
That is what poet Hannah Flagg Gould (1789‑1865) must have been thinking when she wrote a poem called The Frost. Here are parts of that poem.
I’ll be fine. I’m wearing my new coat. That it is NOT a winter coat. I mean, it looks great, but it’s much too thin.

content.introduction: Read "Sometimes We 'Spread Ourself Too Thin'" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Many people have family and work responsibilities. We might have religious or community activities. Taking care of our health is also important. And let’s not forget about our personal projects, our interests, and our friendships.
prompt.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as something people might have in their lives?

prompt.passage: If we work on many projects simultaneously, or at the same time, it is possible that none will get done well, if they get done at all. And it is possible such multitasking could stress us out.
prompt.question: What can happen when we try many projects at once?

prompt.passage: Being spread too thin can also be described as biting off more than we can chew. When we bite off more than we can chew, we attempt to do more than is possible.
prompt.question: What does "bite off more than we can chew" mean?

prompt.passage: A: So, how are your classes going? B: Not great. Many of my best students are failing my course. They all missed an important deadline for a project.
prompt.question: Why are the students failing?
options: They are too busy with a play

prompt.passage: B: Well, first I’ll extend the deadline. And then I’m going to warn them about biting off more than they can chew.
prompt.question: What will the teacher do next?

title: Listening Practice (Generated)
description: LLM-generated listening comprehension drills, one lesson per VOA Learning English passage, with the matching audio file.

content.introduction: Listen to "From Monkeys to Potatoes" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: To monkey around means to do things that are not useful or serious, or to simply waste time.
prompt.question: What does "to monkey around" mean?

prompt.transcript: If you are as sick as a dog you are really, really sick and will most likely stay home from work or school.
prompt.question: What does the phrase "sick as a dog" mean?

prompt.transcript: If you let the cat out of the bag, you spoil a surprise.
prompt.question: What happens if you "let the cat out of the bag"?

prompt.transcript: Some food expressions have good meanings, while others don’t. For example, being a couch potato is not a good thing.
prompt.question: What does "couch potato" describe?
options: a person who likes to cook
a person who watches TV all day

answer_key.answer: a person who watches TV all day

prompt.transcript: The expression "the apple doesn’t fall far from the tree" means that a child acts or looks a lot like the parents.
prompt.question: When do we use "the apple doesn’t fall far from the tree"?
options: when a child is new to a job
when a child looks like the parents
when a child is very smart
when a child is very quiet

answer_key.answer: when a child looks like the parents

lessons.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

content.introduction: Listen to "'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: We use this expression to describe an experience that is uninteresting. Another good word to describe it is tedious.
prompt.question: The expression 'like watching grass grow' is used to describe an experience that is ______.

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.transcript: After the politician won his campaign, he gave a two-hour long acceptance speech. Two hours! It was as much fun as watching grass grow.
prompt.question: According to the transcript, the politician's speech was described as ______.
options: as much fun as watching grass grow
as exciting as watching grass grow
as long as watching grass grow
as beautiful as watching grass grow

answer_key.answer: as much fun as watching grass grow

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.transcript: For people who are always active, always moving, always on-the-go, we can say they don't let grass grow under their feet.
prompt.question: What can we say about people who are always active?
options: they let grass grow under their feet
they don't let grass grow under their feet

answer_key.answer: they don't let grass grow under their feet

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.transcript: If you stand in one place for a long time, the grass will continue to grow under your feet.
prompt.question: What happens to the grass if you stand in one place for a long time?
options: the grass will continue to grow under your feet

answer_key.answer: the grass will continue to grow under your feet

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.transcript: Anna Matteo wrote this story for VOA Learning English.
prompt.question: Who wrote this story for VOA Learning English?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

content.introduction: Listen to "'Kicking Off' a New Year" and answer the questions.

prompt.transcript: One of the most popular sports in the United States is American football. In the U.S., “football” is a sport that involves a lot of contact. What people around the world call “football,” Americans call soccer.
prompt.question: What do Americans call the sport that most people outside the U.S. refer to as 'football'?

prompt.transcript: The term kickoff is commonly used to describe the start of an event or project. For example, we could say experts from all over the world attended the kickoff of the new language museum. You can use it as a phrasal verb, as well. For example, people often like to “kick off” the new year with a resolution.
prompt.question: Which of the following sentences correctly uses the word 'kickoff'?
options: Experts from all over the world attended the kickoff of the new language museum.
I will kickoff the meeting in five minutes.
Kickoff is a kind of football.

answer_key.answer: Experts from all over the world attended the kickoff of the new language museum.

prompt.transcript: Football also has the term “punt.” Punting is another sort of kick a team uses when they cannot score. The punter kicks a ball with the top of his foot. “Punt,” like “kick,” can be both a noun and a verb.
prompt.question: In American football, a 'punt' is:

prompt.transcript: In this case, your boss has moved the goalpost. ... So, if you say that someone has “moved the goalposts,” you are accusing them of changing the rules or end goals to make it difficult for you to succeed.
prompt.question: If someone 'moves the goalposts', what are they doing?
options: changing the rules to make it harder

answer_key.answer: changing the rules to make it harder

prompt.transcript: To do this, the kicker must kick the football between the goalposts at either end of the football field. If he succeeds, his team gets three points.
prompt.question: How many points does a successful field goal give the team?

title: Writing Practice (Generated)
description: LLM-generated sentence-correction and fill-in-the-blank drills, each grounded on one B1 grammar point.

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: there+AUX+be, You are, MODAL/AUX: may

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: There ______ a lot of books on the shelf.
prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct form of the verb to complete the sentence.

prompt.sentence: There is many students in the park.

answer_key.answer: There are many students in the park.

prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct form of 'you' and the verb 'are'.

prompt.sentence: You _______ open the window, because it is very hot inside.
prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct form of the modal verb "may".

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: MODAL/AUX: may, FUNCTIONAL QUESTION: May I ...?, COMPARISON OF EQUALITY: not as/so ... as

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: She may not goes to the meeting if she is sick.

answer_key.answer: She may not go to the meeting if she is sick.

prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct phrase.

prompt.sentence: May I open the window you?
My sister is ______ as tall as I am.
prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct phrase to express equality.
prompt.grammar_point: COMPARISON OF EQUALITY: not as/so ... as

grounded_on.title: COMPARISON OF EQUALITY: not as/so ... as

prompt.sentence: The book is more interesting as the movie.
prompt.grammar_point: COMPARISON OF EQUALITY: not as/so ... as

answer_key.answer: The book is as interesting as the movie.

grounded_on.title: COMPARISON OF EQUALITY: not as/so ... as

lessons.title: Grammar Practice: FUNCTIONAL QUESTION: Won't you ...?, EXCLAMATION: How ADJ/ADV ...!, BE to DO

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: ______ you come to the picnic tomorrow?
prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct phrase.

prompt.sentence: Won't you going to the museum on Saturday?

answer_key.answer: Won't you go to the museum on Saturday?

prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with an adjective to complete the exclamation.

prompt.sentence: The students ______ to present their projects tomorrow.
prompt.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct form of 'be'.

content.introduction: Practice the grammar points covered in this lesson.

prompt.sentence: The team am to win the game.

answer_key.answer: The team is to win the game.
